cards.text: Movement: A change in the position of any object is called movement. Many movements take place in our body and also in other organisms bodies.

Example of movement in the human body:

  1. Movement of eyelids
  2. Movement of the heart muscles
  3. Movement of arms and legs
  4. Movement of head
  5. Movement of teeth and jaw
  6. Movement of neck

Locomotion: When movement results in a change of position of the whole organism, it is called locomotion. Plants are mostly fixed to the ground. So, they do not show locomotion. Animals have the ability to move from one place to another.

Locomotion helps animals in the following ways:

  1. Find their food and water.
  2. Protect themselves from their enemies and predators.
  3. Move from an unfavorable environment to a favorable environment.
  4. Find suitable places for laying eggs or rearing their young ones.

Cartilage

  • Part of the skeleton that is not hard as bones and can be bent, is cartilage.
  • They are found in the upper part of the ear, the tip of the nose, and at the tips of long bones.
The ends of the bones are covered with soft cartilage. It acts as a shock absorber and reduces the fraction between the bones.
The bones are held together at the joints by strong, stretchy bands called ligaments.

The point at which two separate bones meet is called a joint. The joints are strong enough to withstand jerks. The joints are three types, depending on the types of movement they allow- immovable, slightly movable, and freely movable.

Immovable or fixed Joints: These joints do not allow any movement between the bones. For example, the bones of the skull are interlocked with each other and held together by fibrous cartilage which does not allow any movement.

Slightly movable joints: These joints allow very little movement due to the presence of a pad of flexible cartilage between their ends. For example, joints present between adjacent vertebrae in the backbone spine allow only slight movements.
Freely movable joints: most of the joints in the human body are freely movable.

Ball and Socket joint:

  • Ball and socket joint, where the rounded end of one bone fits into the cavity of the other bone.
  • It brings movement in all directions.
  • It is seen in the hips and shoulders of the human body.

Pivot Joint:
  • The joint where our neck joins the heart is a pivot joint.
  • The neck joint allows movement in all directions.
  • One bone rotates over a pivot-like end of the other bone.
  • The skull rotates on the upper end of the backbone.

Hinge Joint:
  • This joint moves like a hinge in one plane only, just like the hinge of a door.
  • Hinge joints usually give sufficient power, because there is less danger of twisting at the joint.

Gliding joint: This kind of joint allows bones to glide over each other, providing little movement in all directions. In our body, the gliding joints are found between the wrist and the carpals and between the ankle and the tarsals.

Skeleton

  • Skeleton is the internal structure in organisms, which helps in bringing about movement.
  • It forms a framework that gives the shape of the body and provides support to organisms.
  • The skeleton is made up of bones.

​​​​​​​Importance of the human skeleton: 

  1. The bones in your body make up a framework called a skeleton.
  2. The skeleton of an adult human being is made up of 206 bones.
  3. A child has many more bones approximately 300 bones in all. But as a child grows into an adult many of the bones fuse together.
  4. Skull protects the brain.
  5. The rib cage protects the heart and lungs.

Function of the skeleton:
  1. It gives shape and support to the body.
  2. It gives protection to the delicate internal organ of the body.
  3. It helps in the movement of your body parts. It helps us to stand, run, walk, etc., together with muscles.
  4. Our bones are filled with a soft material called bones marrow. Red and white blood cells are made inside the bone marrow.
The human skeleton has four basic parts- the skull, the backbone or spine, the ribs and the limbs.

The Skull 
  • It is made up of 22 bones. Out of these 8 flat bones form the brain box or cranium which encloses and protects our brain and 14 bones form the shape of the face.
  • It contains holes for our eyes, ears, nose, and mouth
  • The upper part of the skull is made up of 8 flat bones joined together.
  • The face and jaw contain 14 bones.
  • Our teeth are fixed to jaw bones.

The Backbone or Spine
  • The backbone forms the main axis of the skeleton.
  • The backbone consists of 33 small bones called vertebrae. These small bones join together to form a strong column called the vertebral column.
  • The vertebral column protects the delicate spinal cord.
  • The vertebral column provides the main support to the body.

The rib cage 

  • There are twelve pairs of thin and curved bones called ribs. These bones form a rib cage that protects the heart and the lungs.
  • All 12 pairs of ribs are joined with the backbone at the back and ten pairs are joined with the chest bones at the front.
  • The last two pairs of ribs are free at the front end and are called floating ribs.
  • The rib cage also protects parts of the stomach and the kidneys.

There are two types of gridles:
  1. A pair of shoulder girdles
  2. A pair of hip girdles
The different patterns of movement of animals due to the differences in their skeletal structure are called gaits animals.

Movement of Earthworm
  • The earthworm does not have any internal skeleton.
  • The body is made up of many rings joined end to end and muscles attached to these rings help to extend and shorten the body.
  • The skin of earthworms also has a large number of tiny bristles that help it get a good grip on the ground.
  • Repeated extension and contraction of the body muscles, enable the earthworm to move through the soil.

Movement of Snail

  • Snails move with the help of their muscular, flat foot.
  • They glide along a solid surface that is lubricated with mucus.
  • This motion is powered by succeeding waves of muscular contractions of the foot.

Movement in cockroach: The cockroach is an insect. The body of the cockroach is covered with a hard outer skeleton. This outer skeleton is made up of many units joined together and that permits movement. It has three pairs of legs and two pairs of wings attached to the breast. The cockroach has distinct muscles near the legs which help in walking while the breast muscles move the wings when the cockroach flies.

Movement of Fish
  • Fishes have streamlined body that helps them swim with the least resistance.
  • They use the tail fins for small jerks through water and other fins assist swimming.
  • The tail fin is also used for changing directions.

Movement of Snakes:
  • Snakes do not have legs for movement but use their long backbone along with muscles for movement.
  • Their body curves into many loops, which gives it a forward push by pressing against the ground.

Movement of Birds
  • Birds have special skeletal and muscular structures that help them to fly.
  • The forelimbs are modified to become wings and the bones inside are hollow to suit flying.
  • The bones of the hind limbs are used for perching and walking.
  • The shoulder bones and breastbones are strong and support muscles of flight, which move the wings up and down.
